[DOB-5027] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Barret (continued)
Clinical Notes (continued)
What should I do before I go shopping? · Plan your meals. 
¨ Plan your shopping around healthy meals and recipes that you will prepare for the week. Switch ingredients
in recipes to lower the total fat and calorie content. For example, if a recipe calls for milk, you can add
nonfat or 1% milk instead of whole milk. Use egg substitute in a recipe that calls for whole eggs. Make a list
of the foods you will need for your planned meals. ¨ Plan healthy meals that can be made quickly on days when you are extra busy. Another idea is to plan
meals that you can make in large batches and freeze. You can thaw and eat these meals on days when
you do not have time to cook. For example, spaghetti can be made in several batches at once and frozen. · Find ways to make affordable healthy choices. Look through supermarket ads for sales. Buy fruits and
vegetables when they are in season or buy them at your local farmer's market. · Eat before you go shopping. Shopping on an empty stomach may cause you to buy unhealthy foods because
you feel hungry. What are some tips for making healthy choices while I am shopping? · Know where to find healthy foods in the grocery store. Go to the food sections that are found along the
walls of the store first. Healthy foods, such as fruits, vegetables, bread, dairy products, meat, and fish, are
usually placed in these areas. The inner aisles have other healthy foods, such as canned and frozen fruits and
vegetables, and cereals. However, there are also other less healthy foods in the inner aisles. Some of these
foods include packaged foods, snack foods, and desserts. Shopping in the inner aisles last may help you buy
fewer of these foods. · Read food labels. Use the nutrition information on food labels to help you make healthier food choices when
you shop. 
¨ The serving size is usually listed in cups or pieces and may include a weight (grams, ounces). It also
shows the number of servings that are in a package. Compare the amount that you will eat to the serving
size listed. If the package contains 2 servings and you eat the whole package, then you will eat twice the
amount of calories and nutrients listed. Generated on 1/14/25 9:08 AM Page 512

Clinical Notes (continued)
¨ The nutrients listed on the top section of the label are fat, saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and sodium. Limit these nutrients because eating too much may increase your risk of certain diseases. The total
carbohydrates, fiber, and sugars are also listed. The nutrients you should try to get enough of include
dietary fiber, vitamin A, vitamin C, calcium, and iron. These nutrients may help you to reduce your risk of
diseases such as osteoporosis and heart disease. ¨ The % daily value (DV) listed on the food label next to the nutrients tells you if a food is low or high in
these nutrients. A percent DV of 5% or less means that the food is low in that nutrient. A percent DV of 20%
or more means that the food is high in that nutrient. · Keep food safety in mind. Keep raw, cooked, and ready-to-eat foods separate in your shopping cart. Place
raw seafood, meat, and poultry in plastic bags to prevent leakage of juices to other foods. Foods that need to be
refrigerated or frozen should be taken home and stored within 2 hours. This prevents the growth of bacteria that
can lead to food poisoning. CARE AGREEMENT:
You have the right to help plan your care. Discuss treatment options with your caregivers to decide what care you
want to receive. You always have the right to refuse treatment. The above information is an educational aid only. It is
not intended as medical advice for individual conditions or treatments. Talk to your doctor, nurse or pharmacist before
following any medical regimen to see if it is safe and effective for you. © 2017 Truven Health Analytics LLC Information is for End User's use only and may not be sold, redistributed or
otherwise used for commercial purposes. All illustrations and images included in CareNotes® are the copyrighted
property of A.D.A.M., Inc. or Truven Health Analytics. Carpal Tunnel Syndrome Exercises
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW:
What do I need to know about carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS) exercises? CTS exercises can help relieve pain
and increase your range of motion. Your healthcare provider or physical therapist can tell you how often to do the
exercises. 
What exercises can I do? · Finger extensions: Hold your fingers and thumb close together. Keep them straight. Put a rubber band around
the outside of your fingers and thumb. Spread your fingers apart. Then slowly bring them together without letting
the rubber band fall off. Repeat 40 times.
· Wrist flexor stretch: Hold your arm out straight. Grasp your fingers with your other hand. Slowly bend the
fingers back (palm facing away) until you feel a stretch in your wrist. Hold for 10 seconds. Repeat 5 times. Generated on 1/14/25 9:08 AM Page 513

NORTON PHYSICIANS 
SERVICES

STE 1200
Louisville KY 40218-1929

MRN: EP01934324, DOB: 5/1/1953, Legal Sex: F

[DOB-5025] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Barret (continued)
Clinical Notes (continued)
· Wrist extensor stretch: Hold your arm out straight. Grasp your fingers with your other hand. Slowly bend the
fingers and hand down (palm facing you) until you feel a stretch on top of your hand. Hold for 10 seconds. Repeat 5 times.
· Wrist curls without weights: Sit in a chair with your forearm resting on your thigh or a table. With your palm
facing down, flex your wrist up 3 inches and slowly lower it. Turn your forearm over and repeat with your palm
facing up. Do each exercise 20 times. Generated on 1/14/25 9:08 AM Page 514

Your thyroid is normal. Your vitamin D level is better! I would recommend continuing a vitamin D3 supplement of 2000 IU daily to help with
your levels. Your cholesterol overall looks good. Continue your atorvastatin and Continue working towards a healthy diet and
increasing your physical activity to keep your cholesterol in this range.

Shopping for a Healthy Diet
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW:
Why is shopping for healthy foods important? You may be more likely to follow a healthy meal plan if you have
healthy foods available in your home. A healthy meal plan includes a variety of healthy foods from all the food groups.
It is also low in unhealthy fats, salt, and added sugar. Healthy foods may decrease your risk for heart disease, osteoporosis (brittle bones), and some types of cancer. What is a healthy meal plan? My Plate is a model for planning healthy meals. It shows the types and amounts of
foods that should go on your plate. Fruits and vegetables make up about half of your plate, and grains and protein
make up the other half. A serving of dairy is also included. The amount of calories and serving sizes you need
depends on your age, gender, weight, and height. Examples of healthy foods are listed below: · Eat a variety of vegetables such as dark green, red, and orange vegetables. You can also include canned
vegetables low in sodium (salt) and frozen vegetables without added butter or sauces. · Eat a variety of fresh fruits , canned fruit in 100% juice, frozen fruit, and dried fruit. · Include whole grains. At least half of the grains you eat should be whole grains. Examples include whole
wheat bread, wheat pasta, brown rice, and whole grain cereals such as oatmeal. · Eat a variety of protein foods such as seafood (fish and shellfish), lean meat, and poultry without skin (turkey
and chicken). Examples of lean meats include pork leg, shoulder, or tenderloin, and beef round, sirloin,
tenderloin, and extra lean ground beef. Other protein foods include eggs and egg substitutes, beans, peas, soy
products, nuts, and seeds. · Choose low-fat dairy products such as skim or 1% milk or low-fat yogurt, cheese, and cottage cheese. Generated on 1/14/25 9:08 AM Page 529

Patient Instructions (continued)
What should I do before I go shopping? · Plan your meals. 
¨ Plan your shopping around healthy meals and recipes that you will prepare for the week. Switch ingredients
in recipes to lower the total fat and calorie content. For example, if a recipe calls for milk, you can add
nonfat or 1% milk instead of whole milk. Use egg substitute in a recipe that calls for whole eggs. Make a list
of the foods you will need for your planned meals. ¨ Plan healthy meals that can be made quickly on days when you are extra busy. Another idea is to plan
meals that you can make in large batches and freeze. You can thaw and eat these meals on days when
you do not have time to cook. For example, spaghetti can be made in several batches at once and frozen. · Find ways to make affordable healthy choices. Look through supermarket ads for sales. Buy fruits and
vegetables when they are in season or buy them at your local farmer's market. · Eat before you go shopping. Shopping on an empty stomach may cause you to buy unhealthy foods because
you feel hungry. What are some tips for making healthy choices while I am shopping? · Know where to find healthy foods in the grocery store. Go to the food sections that are found along the
walls of the store first. Healthy foods, such as fruits, vegetables, bread, dairy products, meat, and fish, are
usually placed in these areas. The inner aisles have other healthy foods, such as canned and frozen fruits and
vegetables, and cereals. However, there are also other less healthy foods in the inner aisles. Some of these
foods include packaged foods, snack foods, and desserts. Shopping in the inner aisles last may help you buy
fewer of these foods. · Read food labels. Use the nutrition information on food labels to help you make healthier food choices when
you shop. 
¨ The serving size is usually listed in cups or pieces and may include a weight (grams, ounces). It also
shows the number of servings that are in a package. Compare the amount that you will eat to the serving
size listed. If the package contains 2 servings and you eat the whole package, then you will eat twice the
amount of calories and nutrients listed. Generated on 1/14/25 9:08 AM Page 530

Patient Instructions (continued)
¨ The nutrients listed on the top section of the label are fat, saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and sodium. Limit these nutrients because eating too much may increase your risk of certain diseases. The total
carbohydrates, fiber, and sugars are also listed. The nutrients you should try to get enough of include
dietary fiber, vitamin A, vitamin C, calcium, and iron. These nutrients may help you to reduce your risk of
diseases such as osteoporosis and heart disease. ¨ The % daily value (DV) listed on the food label next to the nutrients tells you if a food is low or high in
these nutrients. A percent DV of 5% or less means that the food is low in that nutrient. A percent DV of 20%
or more means that the food is high in that nutrient. · Keep food safety in mind. Keep raw, cooked, and ready-to-eat foods separate in your shopping cart. Place
raw seafood, meat, and poultry in plastic bags to prevent leakage of juices to other foods. Foods that need to be
refrigerated or frozen should be taken home and stored within 2 hours. This prevents the growth of bacteria that
can lead to food poisoning. CARE AGREEMENT:

WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW:
What do I need to know about carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS) exercises? CTS exercises can help relieve pain
and increase your range of motion. Your healthcare provider or physical therapist can tell you how often to do the
exercises. 
What exercises can I do? · Finger extensions: Hold your fingers and thumb close together. Keep them straight. Put a rubber band around
the outside of your fingers and thumb. Spread your fingers apart. Then slowly bring them together without letting
the rubber band fall off. Repeat 40 times.

Patient Instructions (continued) · Wrist flexor stretch: Hold your arm out straight. Grasp your fingers with your other hand. Slowly bend the
fingers back (palm facing away) until you feel a stretch in your wrist. Hold for 10 seconds. Repeat 5 times.
· Wrist extensor stretch: Hold your arm out straight. Grasp your fingers with your other hand. Slowly bend the
fingers and hand down (palm facing you) until you feel a stretch on top of your hand. Hold for 10 seconds. Repeat 5 times.

Patient Instructions (continued)
· Wrist curls without weights: Sit in a chair with your forearm resting on your thigh or a table. With your palm
facing down, flex your wrist up 3 inches and slowly lower it. Turn your forearm over and repeat with your palm
facing up. Do each exercise 20 times.
· Shrugs: Stand with your arms by your side. Lift your shoulders up to your ears and hold for 1 second. Then
pull your shoulders back, pinching your shoulder blades together. Hold for 1 second. Then relax your shoulders. Repeat 20 times. Generated on 1/14/25 9:08 AM Page 533

The SARS-CoV-2 assay is a real-time RT-PCR test intended for the
qualitative detection of nucleic acid from the SARS-CoV-2 virus in
respiratory specimens from individuals. Testing is limited to the
guidelines of FDA Emergency Use Authorization for performing
SARS-CoV-2 testing. Bluewater Dx Laboratory is CLIA accredited and is qualified as
high-complexity. This testing should be used for clinical purposes
and is not regarded as investigational or for research. Negative (Not Detected) results do not preclude viral infection
below the limit of detection or subsequent infection. These results
should not be used as the sole basis for patient management
decisions. Negative results must be combined with clinical
observations, patient history, and epidemiological information. Reason for Testing OUTPATIEN

For Your Visit: -Will check labs today for blood counts, kidney and liver function, cholesterols, and screen for diabetes. Will notify you
via MyNortonChart or a letter. Lab results will post to MyChart after 48 hours, they will be accompanied by my
comments within 1 week of your lab results, I need time to review them. -Not only do we like to see you when you are well, we want to see you to help take care of you when you are sick. If in
the future, you are not feeling well and need to be seen urgently, please call our office. There are always same day
appointment slots available. For your use of MyChart Messages: 
For urgent issues – chest pain, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, back/joint pain-please call the office for
an appointment. 
Please keep in mind, MyChart Messages are not considered urgent. It will take 2-3 days to get a response back, especially with the pandemic. I know you may be nervous about what is going on. We are doing our best to get back to
you, and sending one message about your issues will help us in that process. To help with your internet research:
-National Institute of Health

-Wear your seatbelt regularly

Shopping for a Healthy Diet
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW:
Why is shopping for healthy foods important? You may be more likely to follow a healthy meal plan if you have
healthy foods available in your home. A healthy meal plan includes a variety of healthy foods from all the food groups.
It is also low in unhealthy fats, salt, and added sugar. Healthy foods may decrease your risk for heart disease, osteoporosis (brittle bones), and some types of cancer. What is a healthy meal plan? My Plate is a model for planning healthy meals. It shows the types and amounts of
foods that should go on your plate. Fruits and vegetables make up about half of your plate, and grains and protein
make up the other half. A serving of dairy is also included. The amount of calories and serving sizes you need
depends on your age, gender, weight, and height. Examples of healthy foods are listed below: · Eat a variety of vegetables such as dark green, red, and orange vegetables. You can also include canned
vegetables low in sodium (salt) and frozen vegetables without added butter or sauces. · Eat a variety of fresh fruits , canned fruit in 100% juice, frozen fruit, and dried fruit. · Include whole grains. At least half of the grains you eat should be whole grains. Examples include whole
wheat bread, wheat pasta, brown rice, and whole grain cereals such as oatmeal. · Eat a variety of protein foods such as seafood (fish and shellfish), lean meat, and poultry without skin (turkey
and chicken). Examples of lean meats include pork leg, shoulder, or tenderloin, and beef round, sirloin,
tenderloin, and extra lean ground beef. Other protein foods include eggs and egg substitutes, beans, peas, soy
products, nuts, and seeds. · Choose low-fat dairy products such as skim or 1% milk or low-fat yogurt, cheese, and cottage cheese.
What should I do before I go shopping? · Plan your meals. 
¨ Plan your shopping around healthy meals and recipes that you will prepare for the week. Switch ingredients
in recipes to lower the total fat and calorie content. For example, if a recipe calls for milk, you can add

Clinical Notes (continued)
nonfat or 1% milk instead of whole milk. Use egg substitute in a recipe that calls for whole eggs. Make a list
of the foods you will need for your planned meals. ¨ Plan healthy meals that can be made quickly on days when you are extra busy. Another idea is to plan
meals that you can make in large batches and freeze. You can thaw and eat these meals on days when
you do not have time to cook. For example, spaghetti can be made in several batches at once and frozen. · Find ways to make affordable healthy choices. Look through supermarket ads for sales. Buy fruits and
vegetables when they are in season or buy them at your local farmer's market. · Eat before you go shopping. Shopping on an empty stomach may cause you to buy unhealthy foods because
you feel hungry. What are some tips for making healthy choices while I am shopping? · Know where to find healthy foods in the grocery store. Go to the food sections that are found along the
walls of the store first. Healthy foods, such as fruits, vegetables, bread, dairy products, meat, and fish, are
usually placed in these areas. The inner aisles have other healthy foods, such as canned and frozen fruits and
vegetables, and cereals. However, there are also other less healthy foods in the inner aisles. Some of these
foods include packaged foods, snack foods, and desserts. Shopping in the inner aisles last may help you buy
fewer of these foods. · Read food labels. Use the nutrition information on food labels to help you make healthier food choices when
you shop. 
¨ The serving size is usually listed in cups or pieces and may include a weight (grams, ounces). It also
shows the number of servings that are in a package. Compare the amount that you will eat to the serving
size listed. If the package contains 2 servings and you eat the whole package, then you will eat twice the
amount of calories and nutrients listed. ¨ The nutrients listed on the top section of the label are fat, saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and sodium. Limit these nutrients because eating too much may increase your risk of certain diseases. The total
carbohydrates, fiber, and sugars are also listed. The nutrients you should try to get enough of include
dietary fiber, vitamin A, vitamin C, calcium, and iron. These nutrients may help you to reduce your risk of
diseases such as osteoporosis and heart disease. ¨ The % daily value (DV) listed on the food label next to the nutrients tells you if a food is low or high in
these nutrients. A percent DV of 5% or less means that the food is low in that nutrient. A percent DV of 20%
or more means that the food is high in that nutrient. · Keep food safety in mind. Keep raw, cooked, and ready-to-eat foods separate in your shopping cart. Place
raw seafood, meat, and poultry in plastic bags to prevent leakage of juices to other foods. Foods that need to be
refrigerated or frozen should be taken home and stored within 2 hours. This prevents the growth of bacteria that
can lead to food poisoning. CARE AGREEMENT:
You have the right to help plan your care. Discuss treatment options with your caregivers to decide what care you
want to receive. You always have the right to refuse treatment. The above information is an educational aid only. It is
not intended as medical advice for individual conditions or treatments. Talk to your doctor, nurse or pharmacist before
following any medical regimen to see if it is safe and effective for you. © 2017 Truven Health Analytics LLC Information is for End User's use only and may not be sold, redistributed or

Thanks for getting your testing done. Your hemoglobin and blood counts look good, and you are not anemic. Your kidney and liver function look good. Please notify patient. Thanks for getting your testing done. Your hemoglobin and blood counts look good, and you are not anemic. Your kidney and liver function look good. Your electrolytes are normal,your potassium is mildly high, we can recheck this in 3 weeks. Your vitamin D is low. I would recommend a vitamin d3 2000 IU supplement to be taken daily to help with your levels. This is
available over the counter, and you would only need it for about 3-6 months. Your varicella zoster IgG is positive, indicating that you were exposed to chickenpox at some point in the past. That would mean 
its important to get vaccinated for shingles. All Reviewers List

[DOB-4391] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Barret (continued)
Flowsheets (continued)
Patient Instructions
For Your Visit: -Will check labs today for blood counts, kidney and liver function, cholesterols, and screen for diabetes. Will notify you
via MyNortonChart or a letter. Lab results will post to MyChart after 48 hours, they will be accompanied by my
comments within 1 week of your lab results, I need time to review them. -Not only do we like to see you when you are well, we want to see you to help take care of you when you are sick. If in
the future, you are not feeling well and need to be seen urgently, please call our office. There are always same day
appointment slots available. For your use of MyChart Messages: 
For urgent issues – chest pain, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, back/joint pain-please call the office for
an appointment. 
Please keep in mind, MyChart Messages are not considered urgent. It will take 2-3 days to get a response back, especially with the pandemic. I know you may be nervous about what is going on. We are doing our best to get back to
you, and sending one message about your issues will help us in that process. To help with your internet research:
-National Institute of Health
-Websites that end in .GOV or .EDU are valid websites
To Stay Well:
-Wear your seatbelt regularly
-Don't text and drive
-Wear sunscreen, and ensure your chapstick has some SPF as well (Blistex, Carmex, and Chapstick brands contain
some SPF protection)
-Practice safe sex by using condoms to help prevent infections
-Maintain good self-care
-Physical activity at least 30 minutes that gets your heart rate up to keep your heart strong

Shopping for a Healthy Diet
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW:
Why is shopping for healthy foods important? You may be more likely to follow a healthy meal plan if you have
healthy foods available in your home. A healthy meal plan includes a variety of healthy foods from all the food groups.
It is also low in unhealthy fats, salt, and added sugar. Healthy foods may decrease your risk for heart disease, osteoporosis (brittle bones), and some types of cancer. What is a healthy meal plan? My Plate is a model for planning healthy meals. It shows the types and amounts of
foods that should go on your plate. Fruits and vegetables make up about half of your plate, and grains and protein
make up the other half. A serving of dairy is also included. The amount of calories and serving sizes you need
depends on your age, gender, weight, and height. Examples of healthy foods are listed below: · Eat a variety of vegetables such as dark green, red, and orange vegetables. You can also include canned
vegetables low in sodium (salt) and frozen vegetables without added butter or sauces. · Eat a variety of fresh fruits , canned fruit in 100% juice, frozen fruit, and dried fruit. · Include whole grains. At least half of the grains you eat should be whole grains. Examples include whole
wheat bread, wheat pasta, brown rice, and whole grain cereals such as oatmeal. · Eat a variety of protein foods such as seafood (fish and shellfish), lean meat, and poultry without skin (turkey
and chicken). Examples of lean meats include pork leg, shoulder, or tenderloin, and beef round, sirloin,
tenderloin, and extra lean ground beef. Other protein foods include eggs and egg substitutes, beans, peas, soy
products, nuts, and seeds. · Choose low-fat dairy products such as skim or 1% milk or low-fat yogurt, cheese, and cottage cheese.
Generated on 1/14/25 9:08 AM Page 709

NORTON PHYSICIANS

[DOB-4389] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Barret (continued)
Patient Instructions (continued)
What should I do before I go shopping? · Plan your meals. 
¨ Plan your shopping around healthy meals and recipes that you will prepare for the week. Switch ingredients
in recipes to lower the total fat and calorie content. For example, if a recipe calls for milk, you can add
nonfat or 1% milk instead of whole milk. Use egg substitute in a recipe that calls for whole eggs. Make a list
of the foods you will need for your planned meals. ¨ Plan healthy meals that can be made quickly on days when you are extra busy. Another idea is to plan
meals that you can make in large batches and freeze. You can thaw and eat these meals on days when
you do not have time to cook. For example, spaghetti can be made in several batches at once and frozen. · Find ways to make affordable healthy choices. Look through supermarket ads for sales. Buy fruits and
vegetables when they are in season or buy them at your local farmer's market. · Eat before you go shopping. Shopping on an empty stomach may cause you to buy unhealthy foods because
you feel hungry. What are some tips for making healthy choices while I am shopping? · Know where to find healthy foods in the grocery store. Go to the food sections that are found along the
walls of the store first. Healthy foods, such as fruits, vegetables, bread, dairy products, meat, and fish, are
usually placed in these areas. The inner aisles have other healthy foods, such as canned and frozen fruits and
vegetables, and cereals. However, there are also other less healthy foods in the inner aisles. Some of these
foods include packaged foods, snack foods, and desserts. Shopping in the inner aisles last may help you buy
fewer of these foods. · Read food labels. Use the nutrition information on food labels to help you make healthier food choices when
you shop. 
¨ The serving size is usually listed in cups or pieces and may include a weight (grams, ounces). It also
shows the number of servings that are in a package. Compare the amount that you will eat to the serving
size listed. If the package contains 2 servings and you eat the whole package, then you will eat twice the
amount of calories and nutrients listed. Generated on 1/14/25 9:08 AM Page 710

[DOB-4388] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Barret (continued)
Patient Instructions (continued)
¨ The nutrients listed on the top section of the label are fat, saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and sodium. Limit these nutrients because eating too much may increase your risk of certain diseases. The total
carbohydrates, fiber, and sugars are also listed. The nutrients you should try to get enough of include
dietary fiber, vitamin A, vitamin C, calcium, and iron. These nutrients may help you to reduce your risk of
diseases such as osteoporosis and heart disease. ¨ The % daily value (DV) listed on the food label next to the nutrients tells you if a food is low or high in
these nutrients. A percent DV of 5% or less means that the food is low in that nutrient. A percent DV of 20%
or more means that the food is high in that nutrient. · Keep food safety in mind. Keep raw, cooked, and ready-to-eat foods separate in your shopping cart. Place
raw seafood, meat, and poultry in plastic bags to prevent leakage of juices to other foods. Foods that need to be
refrigerated or frozen should be taken home and stored within 2 hours. This prevents the growth of bacteria that
can lead to food poisoning. CARE AGREEMENT:
You have the right to help plan your care. Discuss treatment options with your caregivers to decide what care you
want to receive. You always have the right to refuse treatment. The above information is an educational aid only. It is
not intended as medical advice for individual conditions or treatments. Talk to your doctor, nurse or pharmacist before
following any medical regimen to see if it is safe and effective for you. © 2017 Truven Health Analytics LLC Information is for End User's use only and may not be sold, redistributed or

Annual Wellness Plan:
During the course of the visit the patient was educated and counseled about appropriate screening and preventive
services. The following written plan was given to the patient. Patient Instructions
For Your Visit: -Will check labs today for blood counts, kidney and liver function, cholesterols, and screen for diabetes. Will notify you
via MyNortonChart or a letter. Lab results will post to MyChart after 48 hours, they will be accompanied by my
comments within 1 week of your lab results, I need time to review them. -Not only do we like to see you when you are well, we want to see you to help take care of you when you are sick. If in
the future, you are not feeling well and need to be seen urgently, please call our office. There are always same day
appointment slots available. For your use of MyChart Messages: 
For urgent issues – chest pain, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, back/joint pain-please call the office for
an appointment. 
Please keep in mind, MyChart Messages are not considered urgent. It will take 2-3 days to get a response back, especially with the pandemic. I know you may be nervous about what is going on. We are doing our best to get back to
you, and sending one message about your issues will help us in that process. To help with your internet research:
-National Institute of Health
-Websites that end in .GOV or .EDU are valid websites
To Stay Well:
-Wear your seatbelt regularly
-Don't text and drive
-Wear sunscreen, and ensure your chapstick has some SPF as well (Blistex, Carmex, and Chapstick brands contain
some SPF protection)
-Practice safe sex by using condoms to help prevent infections
-Maintain good self-care
-Physical activity at least 30 minutes that gets your heart rate up to keep your heart strong
To do list: 
1. Will check Vitamin D level and then let you know if we need to do any other vitamin D
2. To help with cough:
Montelukast 10 mg by mouth at night to help with cough
3. See eye doctor
4. Stop the hydrochlorothiazide
5. Stop the amlodipine

Clinical Notes (continued)
Shopping for a Healthy Diet
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW:
Why is shopping for healthy foods important? You may be more likely to follow a healthy meal plan if you have
healthy foods available in your home. A healthy meal plan includes a variety of healthy foods from all the food groups.
It is also low in unhealthy fats, salt, and added sugar. Healthy foods may decrease your risk for heart disease, osteoporosis (brittle bones), and some types of cancer.
What is a healthy meal plan? My Plate is a model for planning healthy meals. It shows the types and amounts of
foods that should go on your plate. Fruits and vegetables make up about half of your plate, and grains and protein
make up the other half. A serving of dairy is also included. The amount of calories and serving sizes you need
depends on your age, gender, weight, and height. Examples of healthy foods are listed below: · Eat a variety of vegetables such as dark green, red, and orange vegetables. You can also include
canned vegetables low in sodium (salt) and frozen vegetables without added butter or sauces. · Eat a variety of fresh fruits , canned fruit in 100% juice, frozen fruit, and dried fruit. · Include whole grains. At least half of the grains you eat should be whole grains. Examples include
whole wheat bread, wheat pasta, brown rice, and whole grain cereals such as oatmeal. · Eat a variety of protein foods such as seafood (fish and shellfish), lean meat, and poultry without skin
(turkey and chicken). Examples of lean meats include pork leg, shoulder, or tenderloin, and beef round, sirloin,
tenderloin, and extra lean ground beef. Other protein foods include eggs and egg substitutes, beans, peas, soy
products, nuts, and seeds. · Choose low-fat dairy products such as skim or 1% milk or low-fat yogurt, cheese, and cottage cheese. Generated on 1/14/25 9:08 AM Page 829

Clinical Notes (continued)
What should I do before I go shopping? 
1. Plan your meals. 
○ Plan your shopping around healthy meals and recipes that you will prepare for the week. Switch
ingredients in recipes to lower the total fat and calorie content. For example, if a recipe calls for milk, you
can add nonfat or 1% milk instead of whole milk. Use egg substitute in a recipe that calls for whole eggs. Make a list of the foods you will need for your planned meals. ○ Plan healthy meals that can be made quickly on days when you are extra busy. Another idea is to plan
meals that you can make in large batches and freeze. You can thaw and eat these meals on days when
you do not have time to cook. For example, spaghetti can be made in several batches at once and frozen. 2. Find ways to make affordable healthy choices. Look through supermarket ads for sales. Buy fruits
and vegetables when they are in season or buy them at your local farmer's market. 3. Eat before you go shopping. Shopping on an empty stomach may cause you to buy unhealthy foods because
you feel hungry. What are some tips for making healthy choices while I am shopping? 
1. Know where to find healthy foods in the grocery store. Go to the food sections that are found
along the walls of the store first. Healthy foods, such as fruits, vegetables, bread, dairy products, meat, and fish, are usually placed in these areas. The inner aisles have other healthy foods, such as canned and frozen fruits
and vegetables, and cereals. However, there are also other less healthy foods in the inner aisles. Some of these
foods include packaged foods, snack foods, and desserts. Shopping in the inner aisles last may help you buy
fewer of these foods. 2. Read food labels. Use the nutrition information on food labels to help you make healthier food choices
when you shop. ○ The serving size is usually listed in cups or pieces and may include a weight (grams, ounces). It also
shows the number of servings that are in a package. Compare the amount that you will eat to the serving
size listed. If the package contains 2 servings and you eat the whole package, then you will eat twice the

Clinical Notes (continued)
amount of calories and nutrients listed. ○ The nutrients listed on the top section of the label are fat, saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and
sodium. Limit these nutrients because eating too much may increase your risk of certain diseases. The
total carbohydrates, fiber, and sugars are also listed. The nutrients you should try to get enough of include
dietary fiber, vitamin A, vitamin C, calcium, and iron. These nutrients may help you to reduce your risk of
diseases such as osteoporosis and heart disease. ○ The % daily value (DV) listed on the food label next to the nutrients tells you if a food is low or high in
these nutrients. A percent DV of 5% or less means that the food is low in that nutrient. A percent DV of
20% or more means that the food is high in that nutrient. 3. Keep food safety in mind. Keep raw, cooked, and ready-to-eat foods separate in your shopping cart. Place
raw seafood, meat, and poultry in plastic bags to prevent leakage of juices to other foods. Foods that need to be
refrigerated or frozen should be taken home and stored within 2 hours. This prevents the growth of bacteria that
can lead to food poisoning. CARE AGREEMENT:
You have the right to help plan your care. Discuss treatment options with your healthcare provider to decide what care
you want to receive. You always have the right to refuse treatment. The above information is an educational aid only. It
is not intended as medical advice for individual conditions or treatments. Talk to your doctor, nurse or pharmacist
before following any medical regimen to see if it is safe and effective for you. © Copyright IBM Corporation 2021 Information is for End User's use only and may not be sold, redistributed or

Patient Instructions
For Your Visit: -Will check labs today for blood counts, kidney and liver function, cholesterols, and screen for diabetes. Will notify you
via MyNortonChart or a letter. Lab results will post to MyChart after 48 hours, they will be accompanied by my
comments within 1 week of your lab results, I need time to review them. -Not only do we like to see you when you are well, we want to see you to help take care of you when you are sick. If in
the future, you are not feeling well and need to be seen urgently, please call our office. There are always same day
appointment slots available. For your use of MyChart Messages: 
For urgent issues – chest pain, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, back/joint pain-please call the office for
an appointment. 
Please keep in mind, MyChart Messages are not considered urgent. It will take 2-3 days to get a response back, especially with the pandemic. I know you may be nervous about what is going on. We are doing our best to get back to
you, and sending one message about your issues will help us in that process. To help with your internet research:
-National Institute of Health
-Websites that end in .GOV or .EDU are valid websites
To Stay Well:
-Wear your seatbelt regularly
-Don't text and drive
-Wear sunscreen, and ensure your chapstick has some SPF as well (Blistex, Carmex, and Chapstick brands contain
some SPF protection)
-Practice safe sex by using condoms to help prevent infections
-Maintain good self-care
-Physical activity at least 30 minutes that gets your heart rate up to keep your heart strong

Shopping for a Healthy Diet
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW:
Why is shopping for healthy foods important? You may be more likely to follow a healthy meal plan if you have
healthy foods available in your home. A healthy meal plan includes a variety of healthy foods from all the food groups.
It is also low in unhealthy fats, salt, and added sugar. Healthy foods may decrease your risk for heart disease, osteoporosis (brittle bones), and some types of cancer.
What is a healthy meal plan? My Plate is a model for planning healthy meals. It shows the types and amounts of
foods that should go on your plate. Fruits and vegetables make up about half of your plate, and grains and protein
make up the other half. A serving of dairy is also included. The amount of calories and serving sizes you need
depends on your age, gender, weight, and height. Examples of healthy foods are listed below: · Eat a variety of vegetables such as dark green, red, and orange vegetables. You can also include
canned vegetables low in sodium (salt) and frozen vegetables without added butter or sauces. · Eat a variety of fresh fruits , canned fruit in 100% juice, frozen fruit, and dried fruit. Generated on 1/14/25 9:08 AM Page 858

NORTON PHYSICIANS 
SERVICES
1930 Bishop Ln
STE 1200
Louisville KY 40218-1929
Colvin, Colleen Marie
MRN: EP01934324, DOB: 5/1/1953, Legal Sex: F
Visit date: 11/5/2021
[DOB-3816] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Barret (continued)
Patient Instructions (continued) · Include whole grains. At least half of the grains you eat should be whole grains. Examples include
whole wheat bread, wheat pasta, brown rice, and whole grain cereals such as oatmeal. · Eat a variety of protein foods such as seafood (fish and shellfish), lean meat, and poultry without skin
(turkey and chicken). Examples of lean meats include pork leg, shoulder, or tenderloin, and beef round, sirloin,
tenderloin, and extra lean ground beef. Other protein foods include eggs and egg substitutes, beans, peas, soy
products, nuts, and seeds. · Choose low-fat dairy products such as skim or 1% milk or low-fat yogurt, cheese, and cottage cheese.
What should I do before I go shopping? 
1. Plan your meals. 
○ Plan your shopping around healthy meals and recipes that you will prepare for the week. Switch
ingredients in recipes to lower the total fat and calorie content. For example, if a recipe calls for milk, you
can add nonfat or 1% milk instead of whole milk. Use egg substitute in a recipe that calls for whole eggs. Make a list of the foods you will need for your planned meals. ○ Plan healthy meals that can be made quickly on days when you are extra busy. Another idea is to plan
meals that you can make in large batches and freeze. You can thaw and eat these meals on days when
you do not have time to cook. For example, spaghetti can be made in several batches at once and frozen. 2. Find ways to make affordable healthy choices. Look through supermarket ads for sales. Buy fruits
and vegetables when they are in season or buy them at your local farmer's market. 3. Eat before you go shopping. Shopping on an empty stomach may cause you to buy unhealthy foods because
you feel hungry. What are some tips for making healthy choices while I am shopping? 
1. Know where to find healthy foods in the grocery store. Go to the food sections that are found
along the walls of the store first. Healthy foods, such as fruits, vegetables, bread, dairy products, meat, and fish, are usually placed in these areas. The inner aisles have other healthy foods, such as canned and frozen fruits
Generated on 1/14/25 9:08 AM Page 859

NORTON PHYSICIANS 
SERVICES
1930 Bishop Ln
STE 1200
Louisville KY 40218-1929
Colvin, Colleen Marie

[DOB-3815] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Barret (continued)
Patient Instructions (continued)
and vegetables, and cereals. However, there are also other less healthy foods in the inner aisles. Some of these
foods include packaged foods, snack foods, and desserts. Shopping in the inner aisles last may help you buy
fewer of these foods. 2. Read food labels. Use the nutrition information on food labels to help you make healthier food choices
when you shop. ○ The serving size is usually listed in cups or pieces and may include a weight (grams, ounces). It also
shows the number of servings that are in a package. Compare the amount that you will eat to the serving
size listed. If the package contains 2 servings and you eat the whole package, then you will eat twice the
amount of calories and nutrients listed. ○ The nutrients listed on the top section of the label are fat, saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and
sodium. Limit these nutrients because eating too much may increase your risk of certain diseases. The
total carbohydrates, fiber, and sugars are also listed. The nutrients you should try to get enough of include
dietary fiber, vitamin A, vitamin C, calcium, and iron. These nutrients may help you to reduce your risk of
diseases such as osteoporosis and heart disease. ○ The % daily value (DV) listed on the food label next to the nutrients tells you if a food is low or high in
these nutrients. A percent DV of 5% or less means that the food is low in that nutrient. A percent DV of
20% or more means that the food is high in that nutrient. 3. Keep food safety in mind. Keep raw, cooked, and ready-to-eat foods separate in your shopping cart. Place
raw seafood, meat, and poultry in plastic bags to prevent leakage of juices to other foods. Foods that need to be
refrigerated or frozen should be taken home and stored within 2 hours. This prevents the growth of bacteria that
can lead to food poisoning. CARE AGREEMENT:
You have the right to help plan your care. Discuss treatment options with your healthcare provider to decide what care
you want to receive. You always have the right to refuse treatment. The above information is an educational aid only. It

[DOB-3667] 12:35 PM by Colvin, Colleen Marie "Colleen Colvin" Thanks for getting your testing done. Your hemoglobin and blood counts look good, and you are not anemic. levels. This is available over the counter, and you would only need it for about 3-6 months. Your cholesterol overall looks good. Continue your atorvastatin and Continue working towards a healthy diet and

Question regarding LIPID PANEL
From

To
Colvin, Colleen Marie "Colleen Colvin" Sent and Delivered11/12/2021 8:58 AM
Last Read in MyChart
8/29/2022 6:43 PM by Colvin, Colleen Marie "Colleen Colvin" From my message on your labs:
Thanks for getting your testing done. Your hemoglobin and blood counts look good, and you are not anemic. Your kidney and liver function look good. Your electrolytes are normal. Your thyroid is normal. Your vitamin D is low. I would recommend a vitamin d3 2000 IU supplement to be taken daily to help with your
levels. This is available over the counter, and you would only need it for about 3-6 months. Your cholesterol overall looks good. Continue your atorvastatin and Continue working towards a healthy diet and 
increasing your physical activity to keep your cholesterol in this range. You do have a urinary tract infection. It grew out proteus which is a common bacteria that can cause urinary tract 
infections. It is only sensitive to 3 antibiotics. I was going to recommend doing augmentin 875/125 mg by mouth
twice daily x 5 days. Thanks, 
Dr. Tailor

1/1/2022:
Colleen M Colvin is a 68 y.o. female who presents to the emergency department today with complaints of shortness of
breath. The patient reports that she tested positive for COVID-19 on [DOB-3472] in the BHH ED. She notes that she
had been feeling ill with headache, congestion, chills, sore throat, and non-productive cough 3-4 days prior to this ED
visit. The patient states that her sore throat has improved with time, but that she is now feeling progressively more
short of breath. The patient denies any vomiting. The patient denies a known history of COPD or diabetes mellitus, but does report
that she has a leaky heart valve and that she had one coronary stent placed in the past. She also states that she has a
history of atrial fibrillation. She denies being on any blood thinning medications. She notes that she forgot to take her
Toprol XL (25mg) this evening. The patient is a former smoker with end date in 1987. She drinks alcohol occasionally
but denies drug use. There are no other acute complaints at this time. Generated on 1/14/25 9:08 AM Page 968

Assessment
Colleen was seen today for abdominal pain and shortness of breath. -COVID + on 12/30
-Patient continues to have a lot of cough and shortness of breath. She also has a lot of continued congestion and was
found to have sinusitis now after dealing with this infection for over 13 days. She does have the Breo at home which is
helped with her breathing. We discussed washing mouth out after usage. We also discussed doing Augmentin in the
morning in the evening for 10 days to help with inflammation in the sinuses. Advised patient to use Flonase twice
daily.
Advised patient that if she does not receive the medications to please let us know.
In regards to A. fib I suspect that she had a flare given that she was having an upper respiratory infection advised
patient that this is probably more likely paroxysmal A. fib that is coming and going. We will resend Eliquis.
In regards to domestic situation patient does feel safe at home at this time but advised patient that she does not
deserve that type of verbal abuse. Recommended to patient to do what is best for her and her family. Advised patient
to work on finding a new place to live. And provided support

Patient Instructions
To help with sinus infection: -Use nasal saline for each nostril in the morning to help with congestion ($3.00-$4 generic over the counter)
-Following nasal saline, use flonase (fluticasone) 2 sprays in each nostril in the morning and in the evening to help
with drainage/inflammation in the nose
-Augmentin 875/125 mg by mouth in the morning and in the evening to treat sinus infection and lung infection x 10
days 
Medrol dose pack take as directed
-->Steroids will help relieve the inflammation. Steroids will make you hungrier than usual, sometimes cause you
difficulty in sleeping, or make you irritable. It also increases your blood sugar. For your breathing: -Breo 1 puff daily To help with your breathing
-Albuterol 2 puffs use every 6 hours to help with breathing and cough
>>if they do not give you the albuterol, L shaped inhaler, please call me. Sinusitis

[DOB-3486] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Barret (continued)
Patient Instructions (continued)
What is sinusitis? Sinusitis is inflammation or infection of your sinuses. It is most often caused by a virus. Acute
sinusitis may last up to 12 weeks. Chronic sinusitis lasts longer than 12 weeks. Recurrent sinusitis means you have 4
or more times in 1 year. What increases my risk for sinusitis? · Medical conditions, such as an upper respiratory infection, allergies, asthma, or cystic fibrosis
· Dental infections or procedures, such as gum infections, tooth decay, or a root canal · Smoking
· Abnormal sinus structure, such as nasal growths, swollen tonsils, or a deviated septum
· A weak immune system, from diseases such as diabetes or HIV
What are the signs and symptoms of sinusitis? · Fever · Pain, pressure, redness, or swelling around the forehead, cheeks, or eyes
· Thick yellow or green discharge from your nose
· Tenderness when you touch your face over your sinuses
· Dry cough that happens mostly at night or when you lie down
· Headache and face pain that is worse when you lean forward
· Tooth pain, or pain when you chew
How is sinusitis diagnosed? Your healthcare provider will examine you and ask about your symptoms. He or she
will check inside your nose using a nasal speculum. This is a small tool used to open your nostrils. A sample of the
mucus from your nose may show what germ is causing your infection. How is sinusitis treated? Your symptoms may go away on their own. Your healthcare provider may recommend
watchful waiting for up to 10 days before starting antibiotics. You may need any of the following: · Acetaminophen decreases pain and fever. It is available without a doctor's order. Ask how much to take and
how often to take it. Follow directions. Read the labels of all other medicines you are using to see if they also
contain acetaminophen, or ask your doctor or pharmacist. Acetaminophen can cause liver damage if not taken
correctly. Do not use more than 4 grams (4,000 milligrams) total of acetaminophen in one day. · NSAIDs , such as ibuprofen, help decrease swelling, pain, and fever. This medicine is available with or without a
doctor's order. NSAIDs can cause stomach bleeding or kidney problems in certain people. If you take blood
thinner medicine, always ask your healthcare provider if NSAIDs are safe for you. Always read the medicine
label and follow directions. · Nasal steroid sprays may help decrease inflammation in your nose and sinuses. · Decongestants help reduce swelling and drain mucus in the nose and sinuses. They may help you breathe
easier. · Antihistamines help dry mucus in the nose and relieve sneezing. Generated on 1/14/25 9:08 AM Page 979

[DOB-3487] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Barret (continued)
Patient Instructions (continued) · Antibiotics help treat or prevent a bacterial infection. How can I manage my symptoms? · Rinse your sinuses. Use a sinus rinse device to rinse your nasal passages with a saline (salt water) solution or
distilled water. Do not use tap water. This will help thin the mucus in your nose and rinse away pollen and dirt. It
will also help reduce swelling so you can breathe normally. Ask your healthcare provider how often to do this. · Breathe in steam. Heat a bowl of water until you see steam. Lean over the bowl and make a tent over your
head with a large towel. Breathe deeply for about 20 minutes. Be careful not to get too close to the steam or burn
yourself. Do this 3 times a day. You can also breathe deeply when you take a hot shower. · Sleep with your head elevated. Place an extra pillow under your head before you go to sleep to help your
sinuses drain. · Drink liquids as directed. Ask your healthcare provider how much liquid to drink each day and which liquids
are best for you. Liquids will thin the mucus in your nose and help it drain. Avoid drinks that contain alcohol or
caffeine. · Do not smoke, and avoid secondhand smoke. Nicotine and other chemicals in cigarettes and cigars can
make your symptoms worse. Ask your healthcare provider for information if you currently smoke and need help
to quit. E-cigarettes or smokeless tobacco still contain nicotine. Talk to your healthcare provider before you use
these products. How can I help prevent the spread of germs that cause sinusitis? Wash your hands often with soap and water. Wash your hands after you use the bathroom, change a child's diaper, or sneeze. Wash your hands before you
prepare or eat food. When should I seek immediate care? · Your eye and eyelid are red, swollen, and painful. · You cannot open your eye. · You have vision changes, such as double vision. · Your eyeball bulges out or you cannot move your eye. · You are more sleepy than normal, or you notice changes in your ability to think, move, or talk. · You have a stiff neck, a fever, or a bad headache. · You have swelling of your forehead or scalp. When should I contact my healthcare provider?

Clinical Notes (continued)
Hospital Course:
Colleen M Colvin is a 69 y.o. female former smoker with CAD status post PCI in 2017, thyroid disease, HTN, lupus, OSA, and unclear history of A. fib presents with chest pain, shortness of breath, and palpitations. Pt states she was at
work and her heart "started racing" at approximately 2130. Pt states she has experienced this before but was told she
does not have atrial fibrillation even though it does appear in her chart she thinks she does have. Pt also admits to
dizziness and lightheadedness earlier but not currently. Pt states she took nitroglycerin and it was somewhat
alleviating. Pt states her cardiologist changed her metoprolol prescription from 25 mg to 50 mg last week. Denies
nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain. Patient in the emergency department found to be in A. fib with RVR. proBNP
greater than 1000. Chest x-ray negative for acute cardiopulmonary process. Patient placed on Cardizem drip. Hospitalist service contacted for admission for further evaluation and treatment. Cardiology consulted. Patient started
on Eliquis. Weaned off of Cardizem to p.o. metoprolol. Patient given IV Lasix x1. Echocardiogram demonstrated left- ventricular ejection fraction 58% with borderline left atrial dilation. Patient satting well on room air heart rate controlled. Patient seen and evaluated by myself and the date of discharge and thought stable for discharge home to follow-up
with her primary care provider prior to returning to work. Patient also to follow-up with cardiology in 2 to 3 weeks. DISCHARGE Follow Up Recommendations for labs and diagnostics: As above

Assessment
Colleen Marie Colvin was recently admitted to the hospital. Reviewed recent labs, imaging, procedures done while
inpatient. Reconciled patient's medication list to reflect active medications now. Colleen was seen today for hospital follow up. Diagnoses and all orders for this visit:
Coronary artery disease involving native coronary artery of native heart with angina pectoris (CMS/HCC)
Patient has upcoming scan. I sent a message to Dr. Cherian to let him know that patient was just admitted for
A. fib with RVR. - CMP (Comprehensive Metabolic Panel); Future; Expected date: [DOB-3818]
Persistent atrial fibrillation (CMS/HCC)
Patient had had this previously its been a couple years I think it happened a few years ago with a
hospitalization where she had a bad kidney stone. It sounds like this 1 occurred while she was at work and
has been more persistent patient is currently still in A. fib and I compared this to a Holter monitor from May. 
Advised patient to continue the metoprolol 25 mg XL twice a day. Advised patient to continue Eliquis.

Advised patient that was good for as a blood thinner. - apixaban (ELIQUIS) 5 MG tablet; Take 1 tablet by mouth 2 (two) times daily. Moderate persistent asthma with acute exacerbation
Patient has been having more tightness and shortness of breath. We will increase our Breo to 200 mcg once
a day. I think this will help with the symptoms that she is having think the other aspect of this is that with the 
persistent A. fib she is having some fatigue and some shortness of breath which is likely exacerbated some of 
the asthma as well - BREO ELLIPTA 200-25 MCG/INH AEPB inhaler; Inhale 1 puff into the lungs daily. Thyroid disease
- Thyroid Stimulating Hormone(TSH); Future; Expected date: [DOB-3820]
Postmenopausal
We are due for a bone density so we will do that today
- DXA Bone Density Screening; Future; Expected date: [DOB-3821]
Kidney stones
The other underlying issues that patient was supposed to have a procedure with the urologist for a staghorn
kidney stone. That is been on hold until she can get the PET scan but now we have this persistent A. fib so I
am curious when this procedure will happen. Especially given now that she has to be on Eliquis. I have sent
a message to her cardiologist so that way we can get a better idea of how we can plan
Recommendations to pt: 
To do list: 
1. Want to see your heart rate between 60-100, ideally 60-80
2. To help with the heart rate:
Metoprolol XL 25 mg by mouth in the AM and in the PM
3. To help with the blood thinner: 
Eliquis 5 mg by mouth in the AM and in the PM
4. To help with the breathing:
Breo 200-25 mcg by mouth daily to help with the breathing (yellow)
5. We need to call the sleep medicine doctor- to get an adjustment to the sleep apnea machine
Return in about 3 months (around [DOB-3822]). Electronically signed by Tailor, Monalisa M, MD at 8/10/2022 1:05 PM

Resolved Hospital Problems
No resolved problems to display. Hospital Course 
Hospital Course:
Colleen M Colvin is a 69 y.o. female former smoker with CAD status post PCI in 2017, thyroid disease, HTN, lupus, OSA, and unclear history of A. fib presents with chest pain, shortness of breath, and palpitations. Pt states she was at
work and her heart "started racing" at approximately 2130. Pt states she has experienced this before but was told she
does not have atrial fibrillation even though it does appear in her chart she thinks she does have. Pt also admits to
dizziness and lightheadedness earlier but not currently. Pt states she took nitroglycerin and it was somewhat
alleviating. Pt states her cardiologist changed her metoprolol prescription from 25 mg to 50 mg last week. Denies
nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain. Patient in the emergency department found to be in A. fib with RVR. proBNP
greater than 1000. Chest x-ray negative for acute cardiopulmonary process. Patient placed on Cardizem drip. Hospitalist service contacted for admission for further evaluation and treatment. Cardiology consulted. Patient started
on Eliquis. Weaned off of Cardizem to p.o. metoprolol. Patient given IV Lasix x1. Echocardiogram demonstrated left- ventricular ejection fraction 58% with borderline left atrial dilation. Patient satting well on room air heart rate controlled. Patient seen and evaluated by myself and the date of discharge and thought stable for discharge home to follow-up
with her primary care provider prior to returning to work. Patient also to follow-up with cardiology in 2 to 3 weeks. DISCHARGE Follow Up Recommendations for labs and diagnostics: As above

Imaging (continued)
No answer. VM is full. Will try again later. Please call patient. Message from Dr. Monalisa Tailor: Thanks for getting your testing done. You still have osteopenia or weakened bones. By the FRAX score (10 year risk of fracture), your risk of major fracture is 28%
and your risk of hip fracture is 14%. They do not recommend medications until the risk of major fracture is greater than 20% or
risk of hip fracture is greater than 3%.
I know last time we talked about this, we were considering fosamax as treatment, but you were nervous about the size of the
pill. I was going to suggest boniva (ibrandonate) instead to help with your bone strength, and this would be a pill taken once a
month. The important part following is to take the medication and then stay sitting up for the next 2-3 hours following to digest it
so it doesn't irritate your stomach. Would that be ok?
To prevent this from progressing to osteoporosis, I would recommend a calcium 800 mg supplement + vitamin D3 1000 IU
supplement daily. This can usually be found in a Caltrate-like supplement over the counter. I would also recommend limiting
your alcohol intake to less than 2 drinks per day, and limiting your caffeine intake to less than 2 drinks per day. I would also
recommend increasing your physical activity to help keep your bones strong. We will repeat the DEXA scan in 2 years to see if
your bone strength has improved. (Have not sent medication since I want to see if pt is interested)

Patient Instructions
To help with lung infection: -Use nasal saline for each nostril in the morning to help with congestion ($3.00-$4 generic over the counter)
-Following nasal saline, use flonase (fluticasone) 2 sprays in each nostril in the morning and in the evening to help
with drainage/inflammation in the nose ($7.50-$10 generic over the counter)
-Augmentin 875/125 mg by mouth in the morning and in the evening to treat sinus infection and lung infection x 10
days -Corcidin HBP as a decongestant to help dry you up. For your breathing: 
Medrol dose pack take as directed
-->Steroids will help relieve the inflammation. Steroids will make you hungrier than usual, sometimes cause you
difficulty in sleeping, or make you irritable. It also increases your blood sugar. -Albuterol 2 puffs take as needed to help with shortness of breath *Acute relief* Acute Bronchitis
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW:
What is acute bronchitis? Acute bronchitis is swelling and irritation in the air passages of your lungs. This irritation
may cause you to cough or have other breathing problems. Acute bronchitis often starts because of another illness, such as a cold or the flu. The illness spreads from your nose and throat to your windpipe and airways. Bronchitis is
often called a chest cold. Acute bronchitis lasts about 3 to 6 weeks and is usually not a serious illness. What causes acute bronchitis? · Infection caused by a virus, bacteria, or a fungus
· Polluted air caused by chemical fumes, dust, smoke, allergens, or pollution
What increases my risk for acute bronchitis? · Age, usually older adults
· Smoking cigarettes or being around cigarette smoke
· Chronic lung diseases or chronic sinus infections
Generated on 1/14/25 9:08 AM Page 1213

NORTON PHYSICIANS 
SERVICES
1930 Bishop Ln

Louisville KY 40218-1929
Colvin, Colleen Marie

Visit date: 5/17/2023
[DOB-4424] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Barret (continued)
Patient Instructions (continued) · Weakened immune system
· Gastroesophageal reflux disease
· Allergies and environmental changes
What are the signs and symptoms of acute bronchitis? · A cough with sputum that may be clear, yellow, or green
· Feeling more tired than usual, and body aches
· A fever and chills
· Wheezing when you breathe
· A tight chest or pain when you breathe or cough
How is acute bronchitis diagnosed? Your healthcare provider may diagnose bronchitis by your symptoms. If he is
not sure, you may need the following: · Blood tests will be done to see if your symptoms are caused by an infection. · X-ray pictures of your lungs and heart may show signs of infection, such as pneumonia. Chest x-rays may also
show fluid around your heart and lungs. How is acute bronchitis treated? Your healthcare provider will treat any condition that has caused your acute
bronchitis. He may also give you any of the following: · Ibuprofen or acetaminophen are medicines that help lower your fever. They are available without a doctor's
order. Ask your healthcare provider which medicine is right for you. Ask how much to take and how often to take
it. Follow directions. These medicines can cause stomach bleeding if not taken correctly. Ibuprofen can cause
kidney damage. Do not take ibuprofen if you have kidney disease, an ulcer, or allergies to aspirin. Acetaminophen can cause liver damage. Do not take more than 4,000 milligrams in 24 hours. Generated on 1/14/25 9:08 AM Page 1214

[DOB-4425] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Barret (continued)
Patient Instructions (continued) · Decongestants help loosen mucus in your lungs and make it easier to cough up. This can help you breathe
easier. · Cough suppressants decrease your urge to cough. If your cough produces mucus, do not take a cough
suppressant unless your healthcare provider tells you to. Your healthcare provider may suggest that you take a
cough suppressant at night so you can rest. · Inhalers may be given. Your healthcare provider may give you one or more inhalers to help you breathe easier
and cough less. An inhaler gives your medicine to open your airways. Ask your healthcare provider to show you
how to use your inhaler correctly.
How can I care for myself when I have acute bronchitis? · Get more rest. Rest helps your body to heal. Slowly start to do more each day. Rest when you feel it is
needed. · Avoid irritants in the air. Avoid chemicals, fumes, and dust. Wear a face mask if you must work around dust
or fumes. Stay inside on days when air pollution levels are high. If you have allergies, stay inside when pollen
counts are high. Do not use aerosol products, such as spray-on deodorant, bug spray, and hair spray. · Do not smoke or be around others who smoke. Nicotine and other chemicals in cigarettes and cigars
damages the cilia that move mucus out of your lungs. Ask your healthcare provider for information if you
currently smoke and need help to quit. E-cigarettes or smokeless tobacco still contain nicotine. Talk to your
Generated on 1/14/25 9:08 AM Page 1215

NORTON PHYSICIANS

[DOB-4426] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Barret (continued)
Patient Instructions (continued)
healthcare provider before you use these products. · Drink liquids as directed. Liquids help keep your air passages moist and help you cough up mucus. You may
need to drink more liquids when you have acute bronchitis. Ask how much liquid to drink each day and which
liquids are best for you. · Use a humidifier or vaporizer. Use a cool mist humidifier or a vaporizer to increase air moisture in your home. This may make it easier for you to breathe and help decrease your cough. How can I decrease my risk for acute bronchitis? · Get the vaccinations you need. Ask your healthcare provider if you should get vaccinated against the flu or
pneumonia. · Prevent the spread of germs. You can decrease your risk of acute bronchitis and other illnesses by doing the
following:
○ Wash your hands often with soap and water. Carry germ-killing hand lotion or gel with you. You can use
the lotion or gel to clean your hands when soap and water are not available. ○ Do not touch your eyes, nose, or mouth unless you have washed your hands first. ○ Always cover your mouth when you cough to prevent the spread of germs. It is best to cough into a
tissue or your shirt sleeve instead of into your hand. Ask those around you cover their mouths when they
cough.
○ Try to avoid people who have a cold or the flu. If you are sick, stay away from others as much as
possible.
When should I seek immediate care? · You cough up blood. · Your lips or fingernails turn blue. · You feel like you are not getting enough air when you breathe. When should I contact my healthcare provider?

appointment slots available. For your use of MyChart Messages:

an appointment. 
Please keep in mind, MyChart Messages are not considered urgent. It will take 2-3 days to get a response back, especially with the pandemic. I know you may be nervous about what is going on. We are doing our best to get back to

like 10 minutes 3 times a day. Also consider weight training a couple of times a week. 4a: Limit alcohol to less than 10 drinks a week. A drink is one beer, 4 ounces of wine, or 1.5 ounces of liquor. 5: Wear your safety belt. 11b: Over-the-counter pain medications should be limited. Heart Healthy Diet
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW:
What is a heart healthy diet? A heart healthy diet is an eating plan low in unhealthy fats and sodium (salt). The plan
is high in healthy fats and fiber. A heart healthy diet helps improve your cholesterol levels and lowers your risk for
heart disease and stroke. A dietitian will teach you how to read and understand food labels. What diet guidelines should I follow? 
1. Choose foods that contain healthy fats. 
○ Unsaturated fats include monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats. Unsaturated fat is found in foods
such as soybean, canola, olive, corn, and safflower oils. It is also found in soft tub margarine that is made
with liquid vegetable oil. ○ Omega-3 fat is found in certain fish, such as salmon, tuna, and trout, and in walnuts and flaxseed. Eat
fish high in omega-3 fats at least 2 times a week.

2. Get 20 to 30 grams of fiber each day. Fruits, vegetables, whole-grain foods, and legumes (cooked
beans) are good sources of fiber.
3. Limit or do not have unhealthy fats. 
○ Cholesterol is found in animal foods, such as eggs and lobster, and in dairy products made from
whole milk. Limit cholesterol to less than 200 mg each day. ○ Saturated fat is found in meats, such as bacon and hamburger. It is also found in chicken or turkey
skin, whole milk, and butter. Limit saturated fat to less than 7% of your total daily calories. ○ Trans fat is found in packaged foods, such as potato chips and cookies. It is also in hard margarine, Generated on 1/14/25 9:08 AM Page 1290

What can I include in my heart healthy plan? Ask your dietitian or healthcare provider how many servings to have
from each of the following food groups:
1. Grains: 
○ Whole-wheat breads, cereals, and pastas, and brown rice
○ Low-fat, low-sodium crackers and chips
2. Vegetables: 
○ Broccoli, green beans, green peas, and spinach
○ Collards, kale, and lima beans
○ Carrots, sweet potatoes, tomatoes, and peppers
○ Canned vegetables with no salt added
3. Fruits: 
○ Bananas, peaches, pears, and pineapple
○ Grapes, raisins, and dates
○ Oranges, tangerines, grapefruit, orange juice, and grapefruit juice

○ Apricots, mangoes, melons, and papaya
○ Raspberries and strawberries

4. Low-fat dairy: 
○ Nonfat (skim) milk, 1% milk, and low-fat almond, cashew, or soy milks fortified with calcium
○ Low-fat cheese, regular or frozen yogurt, and cottage cheese
5. Meats and proteins: 
○ Lean cuts of beef and pork (loin, leg, round), skinless chicken and turkey
○ Legumes, soy products, egg whites, or nuts
What should I limit or not include in my heart healthy plan? 
1. Unhealthy fats and oils: 
○ Whole or 2% milk, cream cheese, sour cream, or cheese
○ High-fat cuts of beef (T-bone steaks, ribs), chicken or turkey with skin, and organ meats such as liver
○ Butter, stick margarine, shortening, and cooking oils such as coconut or palm oil
2. Foods and liquids high in sodium: 
○ Packaged foods, such as frozen dinners, cookies, macaroni and cheese, and cereals with more than
300 mg of sodium per serving
○ Vegetables with added sodium, such as instant potatoes, vegetables with added sauces, or regular
canned vegetables
○ Cured or smoked meats, such as hot dogs, bacon, and sausage
○ High-sodium ketchup, barbecue sauce, salad dressing, pickles, olives, soy sauce, or miso
3. Foods and liquids high in sugar: 
○ Candy, cake, cookies, pies, or doughnuts
○ Soft drinks (soda), sports drinks, or sweetened tea
○ Canned or dry mixes for cakes, soups, sauces, or gravies
What other guidelines should I follow for a healthy heart? · Do not smoke. Nicotine and other chemicals in cigarettes and cigars can cause lung and heart

damage. Ask your healthcare provider for information if you currently smoke and need help to quit. E-cigarettes
or smokeless tobacco still contain nicotine. Talk to your healthcare provider before you use these products. · Limit or do not drink alcohol as directed. Alcohol can damage your heart and raise your blood
pressure. Your healthcare provider may give you specific daily and weekly limits. The general recommended
limit is 1 drink a day for women 21 or older and for men 65 or older. Do not have more than 3 drinks in a day or
7 in a week. The recommended limit is 2 drinks a day for men 21 to 64 years of age. Do not have more than 4
drinks in a day or 14 in a week. A drink of alcohol is 12 ounces of beer, 5 ounces of wine, or 1½ ounces of
liquor. · Exercise regularly. Exercise can help you maintain a healthy weight and improve your blood pressure
and cholesterol levels. Regular exercise can also decrease your risk for heart problems. Ask your healthcare
provider about the best exercise plan for you. Do not start an exercise program without asking your 
healthcare provider.

levels which is available over the counter, Superior Source from amazon is good, or the one from Trader Joe's. This

Your hemoglobin and blood counts look good, and you are not anemic. Your kidney and liver function look good. Your electrolytes are normal. Your thyroid is normal. Your B12 is low-normal. I would recommend Vitamin B12 1000 mcg sublingual tablets daily to help with vitamin b12 levels
which is available over the counter, Superior Source from amazon is good, or the one from Trader Joe's. This can add to the
burning/tingling in your feet. Your cholesterol overall looks good. Continue your atorvastatin and working towards a healthy diet and increasing your physical
activity to keep your cholesterol in this range. Your A1c (3 month marker for diabetes) is in the prediabetic range with an average 3 month blood sugar of 135. This is
considered prediabetes between 5.7-6.4%. >>If pre-diabetic, would initially start a medication called Metformin 500 mg by mouth taken twice daily with meals. It helps your
body better utilize blood sugar and helps bring the blood sugar down. >>I would recommend limiting carbohydrates 25 grams/per meal for women, 35 grams/per meal for men to help with your
glucose. >>I would recommend cutting down on sweets, sodas, alcohol to help with limiting carbohydrates in your nutrition. >>There may also be resources available from your employer to help with prediabetes. Patient Instructions
For Your Visit: -Will check labs today for blood counts, kidney and liver function, cholesterols, and screen for diabetes. Will notify you
via MyNortonChart or a letter. Lab results will post to MyChart after 48 hours, they will be accompanied by my
comments within 1 week of your lab results, I need time to review them. -Not only do we like to see you when you are well, we want to see you to help take care of you when you are sick. If in
the future, you are not feeling well and need to be seen urgently, please call our office. There are always same day
appointment slots available. For your use of MyChart Messages: 
For urgent issues – chest pain, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, back/joint pain-please call the office for
an appointment. 
Please keep in mind, MyChart Messages are not considered urgent. It will take 2-3 days to get a response back, especially with the pandemic. I know you may be nervous about what is going on. We are doing our best to get back to
you, and sending one message about your issues will help us in that process. To help with your internet research:
-National Institute of Health
-Websites that end in .GOV or .EDU are valid websites
To Stay Well:
1b: Get a dental checkup every 6-12 months. 2a: Exercise regularly for your heart: 150 minutes of moderate exercise like brisk walking, or 75 minutes of vigorous
exercise. 30 minutes a day, 5 times a week is an easy goal to remember. You can break this into shorter sessions,
like 10 minutes 3 times a day. Also consider weight training a couple of times a week. 4a: Limit alcohol to less than 10 drinks a week. A drink is one beer, 4 ounces of wine, or 1.5 ounces of liquor. 5: Wear your safety belt. 11b: Over-the-counter pain medications should be limited. They can cause stomach bleeding, and kidney or liver
damage. 
16b: Screening Tests - Colonoscopy should be performed at least every 10 years until the age of 75 (or stool test more
frequently) for Colorectal cancer. 16f: Shots - get a Flu vaccine annually. 16j: Shots - get a Tetanus vaccine every 10 years. To do list: 
1. The eliquis is really, really important - we need to be really regular with this since I don't want you to have a stroke
2. Nasal saline - use that in the AM to help with moisturizing your nose and preventing nose bleeds
3. To help with yoru breathing: 
Stop the breo 
Trelegy 1 puff daily to help with the breathing

Heart Healthy Diet
WHAT YOU NEED TO KNOW:
What is a heart healthy diet? A heart healthy diet is an eating plan low in unhealthy fats and sodium (salt). The plan
is high in healthy fats and fiber. A heart healthy diet helps improve your cholesterol levels and lowers your risk for
heart disease and stroke. A dietitian will teach you how to read and understand food labels. What diet guidelines should I follow? 
1. Choose foods that contain healthy fats. 
○ Unsaturated fats include monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats. Unsaturated fat is found in foods
such as soybean, canola, olive, corn, and safflower oils. It is also found in soft tub margarine that is made
with liquid vegetable oil. ○ Omega-3 fat is found in certain fish, such as salmon, tuna, and trout, and in walnuts and flaxseed. Eat
fish high in omega-3 fats at least 2 times a week.
2. Get 20 to 30 grams of fiber each day. Fruits, vegetables, whole-grain foods, and legumes (cooked
beans) are good sources of fiber. Generated on 1/14/25 9:08 AM Page 1327

NORTON PHYSICIANS 
SERVICES
1930 Bishop Ln
STE 1200
Louisville KY 40218-1929
Colvin, Colleen Marie
MRN: EP01934324, DOB: 5/1/1953, Legal Sex: F
Visit date: 12/5/2023
[DOB-4817] - Office Visit in Norton Community Medical Associates - Barret (continued)
Patient Instructions (continued)
3. Limit or do not have unhealthy fats. 
○ Cholesterol is found in animal foods, such as eggs and lobster, and in dairy products made from
whole milk. Limit cholesterol to less than 200 mg each day. ○ Saturated fat is found in meats, such as bacon and hamburger. It is also found in chicken or turkey
skin, whole milk, and butter. Limit saturated fat to less than 7% of your total daily calories. ○ Trans fat is found in packaged foods, such as potato chips and cookies. It is also in hard margarine, some fried foods, and shortening. Do not eat foods that contain trans fats. 4. Limit sodium as directed. You may be told to limit sodium to 2,000 to 2,300 mg each day. Choose
low-sodium or no-salt-added foods. Add little or no salt to food you prepare. Use herbs and spices in place of
salt.
